        TRANSFER ls_line TO lv_filename.
      ENDLOOP.
      CLOSE DATASET lv_filename.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.

Step 6 — Create Job Catalog & Template in ADT

  1. In ADT (Eclipse) → New → Application Job Catalog Entry
    • Enter your class name (ZCL_MY_APJ_JOB)
    • Give it a Catalog Name (e.g. ZCAT_MY_JOB)
  2. New → Application Job Template
    • Reference the Catalog Name created above
    • Define default parameter values
  3. Schedule via Application Jobs Fiori app (transaction /n/SDF/JOBD or Fiori Launchpad)

entry.title: Fiori tile showing "Target Not Found" error on dynamic tile count — S/4HANA
entry.content:
How to fix the “Target Not Found” error on a Fiori dynamic tile count

Your tile opens fine — but the count is broken. Here’s exactly why it happens and two ways to fix it permanently.


You open the SAP Fiori Launchpad and notice one of your dynamic tiles is showing an error badge on its count — but when you click it, the app loads perfectly. The tile works. The number doesn’t. What’s going on?

This is a surprisingly common issue, and the root cause is almost always the same: the tile’s Dynamic Data Service URL is pointing to the wrong OData service or entity set — one that belongs to a different app entirely.

The tile navigates correctly because its target URL is fine. The count fails because its data service URL is mapped to someone else’s OData.
The scenario

In this example, a “Manage Purchase Orders” tile was misconfigured to fetch its count from MM_PUR_POITEMS_MONI_SRV — which is the OData service for Monitor Purchase Order Items, a completely different app. The network tab confirms it: a $count request to that service returns an error, while the tile’s navigation itself works fine.

Two ways to fix it

Depending on your situation, you have two approaches. If the current OData service is simply misconfigured, fix the URL directly (Option 1). If it’s a deprecated service that SAP no longer pushes updates to, create a fresh dynamic tile with the correct service from scratch (Option 2).

Option 1 :

Step-by-step fix
1 : Capture the failing OData service. Open browser DevTools → Network tab. Click the broken tile. Find the request with $count in the URL that’s returning an error. Copy the OData service name from the request URI — for example, MM_PUR_POITEMS_MONI_SRV.

2: Identify what app that service belongs to. Go to the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library → All Apps. Paste the service name in the search bar. This will tell you which app actually owns that OData service — and confirm it’s not the same app as your tile.

3: Find the tile in the Fiori Launchpad. In the Fiori Launchpad, go to Settings → Groups. Enter the group name where your tile lives, locate the tile, and note its catalog assignment.

4: Open the tile configuration and inspect the Dynamic Data Service URL. In the catalog editor, open the tile. Under Dynamic Data Service URL, you’ll see it pointing to the wrong OData service — that’s the culprit.

 

5: Get the correct OData service from the Fiori App Library. Search for the tile’s actual app name (e.g. “Manage Purchase Orders”) in the Fiori App Library. Under the Configuration section, copy the correct OData service name.

 

6: Verify the service is active in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE. Go to T-code /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, click Filter, enter the correct OData service name, and confirm the status shows Active.
Important: If the service is not active, activate it before proceeding. An inactive service will continue to break the count regardless of any other changes you make.

 

7: Find the correct entity set via SAP Gateway Client. From within /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, select the active service and open the SAP Gateway Client. Browse Entity Sets and find the one that should be used for the count. Copy its full service URI.

 

8: Update the tile’s Dynamic Data Service URL and save. Return to the tile configuration in the catalog. Replace the incorrect service URL with the correct one from the previous step. Save.

 

9: Hard refresh and verify. Press Ctrl + Shift + R (or Cmd + Shift + R on Mac) to hard refresh the Fiori Launchpad. The tile count should now display correctly.

Option 2 :

If the current tile’s OData service is deprecated, fixing the URL is only a partial solution — SAP will not push future updates to deprecated services. The cleaner fix is to retire the old tile and create a new dynamic tile with the correct, current service from the start.

1.Copy configuration from the existing static tile. Open the existing static Manage Purchase Order tile in the catalog and note down all key details: title, subtitle, icon, semantic object, action, and navigation target.

2.Create a new dynamic tile in the catalog. In the Fiori Launchpad catalog, add a new tile and choose the Dynamic tile type. Paste in the configuration values from the static tile.

3.Set the correct Dynamic Data Service URL. In the Dynamic Data Service URL field, enter the correct, active OData service URI — the one identified from the Fiori App Library and verified in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE  This is the only field that differs from the original tile.

 

4.Add the new tile to the group. Go to Settings → Groups in the Fiori Launchpad. Add the newly created dynamic tile to the appropriate group. Remove or hide the old broken tile.

5.Verify in the Fiori Launchpad. Hard refresh the launchpad. The new dynamic tile should display its count correctly without any errors.

When to choose Option 2: If the tile's original service is flagged as deprecated in the Fiori App Library or IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE, go with this approach — it keeps your launchpad aligned with SAP's current service landscape.

Why does this happen?

This misconfiguration typically occurs after a tile is copied or cloned in the launchpad catalog without updating its Dynamic Data Service URL. The navigation target gets updated (so the app opens correctly) but the count service URL is left pointing at whatever the source tile used — often a completely different OData service. It can also happen during system migrations or when business roles are transported across landscapes without a proper review of tile-level configuration details.

Conclusion

Fiori dynamic tile count errors are easy to overlook — the tile itself keeps working, so it can feel like a cosmetic issue. But it's a service misconfiguration with a clear fix once you know where to look.

The Dynamic Data Service URL on your tile must point to the correct, active OData service for that specific app — not any other app in the same catalog or group. If the service is simply wrong, fix it directly (Option 1). If it's deprecated and SAP no longer pushes updates to it, build a fresh dynamic tile with the right service (Option 2).

A quick check in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library and /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE is all it takes to restore the count and keep your Fiori Launchpad accurate for your end users.

entry.title: How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Introduction

If you've built a custom Fiori application on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) and want to expose it as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone, this tutorial walks you through the complete process — from deployment in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to the final working tile in Work Zone.
This guide assumes you already have:
  • A working RAP-based OData service on S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • A Fiori application project in BAS that consumes this service
  • Access to SAP Build Work Zone (standard edition)
  • The app accessible via ADT preview

By the end of this tutorial, your app will be accessible as a tile in your Work Zone site, sitting alongside other tiles like My Inbox.

Architecture Overview

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)
        │
        │  Deploy to ABAP Repository
        ▼
SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 
   ├── OData Service (Service Binding)
   ├── IAM App (External App) + LADI
   ├── Business Catalog
   └── Business Role
        │
        │  Semantic Object + Action
        ▼
SAP Build Work Zone (BTP)
   └── Tile → resolves intent → opens S/4 app via SSO

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system with developer access
  • SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with a destination configured to your S/4 system
  • SAP Build Work Zone subscription on your BTP subaccount
  • A working Fiori app project in BAS (currently accessible via ADT preview only)
  • Both BTP and S/4 trusting the same SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) tenant for SSO

Step 1: Configure crossNavigation in manifest.json

Before deploying, your Fiori app needs to declare how it will be launched from the Fiori Launchpad. This is done via the crossNavigation/inbounds section in manifest.json.
Open webapp/manifest.json and add the following inside the "sap.app" section:
"crossNavigation": {
  "inbounds": {
    "intent1": {
      "semanticObject": "ZObjectName",
      "action": "display",
      "signature": {
        "parameters": {},
        "additionalParameters": "allowed"
      },
      "title": "App Title",
      "subTitle": "SAP Fiori application",
      "icon": "sap-icon://icon_name"
    }
  }
}
Key points:
  • The semanticObject and action together form the intent (#ZObjectName-display) that the Fiori Launchpad and Work Zone will use to navigate to your app.
  • Choose a meaningful semantic object name (prefix with Z for custom).
  • The title here will appear on the tile.
  • To browse available icons for your tile, see the Icon Explorer . 
Important: Also ensure your i18n property in sap.app uses the simple string format:
"i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
Do NOT use the object format: { "bundleName": "..." }

Note: The S/4HANA Cloud LADI converter cannot process the object format for i18n. Use the simple string format instead. For more details on this issue, see:
UI5 CLI - GitHub Issue #432 
SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help 

Your manifest.json should look like: 

 

Step 2: Ensure i18n.properties File Exists

Make sure the file webapp/i18n/i18n.properties exists and contains at minimum:
appTitle=App Title
appDescription=SAP Fiori Application
Without this file, the LADI creation will fail with the error: "File is not a 'properties' file for texts."

Step 3: Configure Deployment to ABAP

In BAS, configure the deployment target. There are two ways to do this:
Option A: Via Application Info (UI-based)
  1. Right-click your project and select "Open Application Info"
  2. Click the "Deploy" option
  3. Fill in the deployment configuration fields
Option B: Via terminal command
npm run deploy-config
This launches the same configuration wizard in the terminal.
 
Both approaches will prompt you to fill in the following fields:

FieldValue
Target TypeABAP
DestinationYour S/4 destination 
SAPUI5 ABAP RepositoryA unique name, max 15 chars (eg: ZREPO_NAME)
Deployment DescriptionAny suitable description 
PackageYour ABAP package
Transport RequestYour transport request (or leave empty for $TMP)

This generates a ui5-deploy.yaml file:

Step 4: Deploy the Application

Run the deployment from the BAS terminal:
npm run deploy
This builds the app and deploys it to the S/4HANA Cloud ABAP repository.

What to look for in the output:

SAPUI5 Application has been uploaded and registered successfully
Updated LADIs for UI5 Repository "", package "", transport ""
Inbound intent1 (ZObjectName-display) was converted to LADI ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
Launchpad App Descriptor Item ZREPO_NAME_UI5R was updated
Deployment Successful.

For example:

 

If you see warnings like:
  • "No LADI was created as no inbound is defined in manifest.json" → You're missing the crossNavigation block
  • "Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s) / File is not a 'properties' file for texts" → Change i18n to the simple string format

Step 5: Create an IAM App (External App) in S/4

After deployment, the LADI exists on the system but isn't yet linked to any access control. You need to create a new IAM App to hold it and grant access to the OData service.
  1. Open your S/4HANA Cloud system
  2. Search for and open the "Create IAM App" application
  3. Create a new IAM App with:
    • App Type: External App
    • IAM App ID: A meaningful name 
  4. In the "Services" section:
    • Add your OData service
    • This grants the app authorization to call the backend service
  5. In the "Fiori Launchpad App Descr Items" section:
    • Add the LADI ID: ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
    • This enables Launchpad intent navigation
  6. Save and publish

 

 

Why a separate IAM App?
Your OData service already has its own IAM App (type: Custom Inbound Service App) for API access. The External App IAM App is specifically for Launchpad navigation.

Step 6: Add IAM App to a Business Catalog

  • Open Maintain Business Catalogs (or create a new catalog in ADT)
  • Open or create your catalog 
  • Add your new External App IAM App to this catalog
  • Publish the catalog

 

 

Step 7: Assign Business Catalog to a Business Role

  • Open Maintain Business Roles
  • Open the Business Role assigned to your users
  • Go to Assigned Business Catalogs
  • Add your catalog 
  • Save

Step 8: Verify on S/4 Fiori Launchpad

Before adding the tile in Work Zone, verify the app works natively on S/4:
https://<your-system>.lab.s4hana.cloud.sap/ui#ZObjectName-display
Your app should open without errors.
If you get "navigation target could not be resolved", double-check:
  • The LADI is added to the IAM App
  • The IAM App is in a Business Catalog
  • The Business Catalog is in your user's Business Role
  • You've logged out and back in after making changes

Step 9: Add the Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Now that the app works on S/4 via intent navigation, add it to Work Zone:
  • Open SAP Build Work Zone administration
  • Go to Content Manager
  • Create a new App
In the Navigation tab:
  • Semantic Object: ZObjectName (same as the one in manifest.json)
  • Action: display
In the Visualization tab:
  • Title, subtitle, icon as desired
  • Save the app

Step 10: Assign to Role and Group

  • In Content Manager → Roles: Open your role and assign the new app
  • In Content Manager → Groups: Open your group (e.g., "SBPA") and add the app
  • Ensure the role is mapped to a Role Collection that your users have

Step 11: Verify the Tile in Work Zone

Open your Work Zone site.
Your new tile should appear in the designated group/section.
Click it — the app opens seamlessly via SSO (since both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant).

Troubleshooting

Issue CauseFix
"No LADI was created" during deployMissing crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.jsonAdd the crossNavigation block and redeploy
"Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s)"i18n uses object formatChange to "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
"Navigation target could not be resolved"LADI not linked to IAM App, or missing catalog/role assignmentAdd LADI to External App IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role
403 Forbidden / UCON blockedTrying to access direct BSP URLUse Launchpad URL (/ui#SemanticObject-action)
Login prompt (username/password) in Work ZoneURL uses -api endpoint, or SSO not configuredUse non-api URL; ensure both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant
"Destination https://https://..." errorDouble protocol in URLRemove the extra https:// prefix

Key Takeaways

  • crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.json is what creates the LADI during deployment.
  • i18n must use the simple string format ("i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties") for the LADI converter to work.
  • A separate IAM App of type External App is needed for the Launchpad tile.
  • The chain must be complete: LADI → IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role → User
  • In Work Zone, use semantic object + action — not URL tiles.
  • SSO works automatically when both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant.

Conclusion

The path from "app works in ADT preview" to "tile in Work Zone" requires a clear understanding of the deployment chain:
  • BAS deploys the UI and creates the LADI
  • The IAM App links the LADI to access control
  • Work Zone resolves the semantic intent
Each piece is simple on its own, but missing any single step results in confusing errors.
Once this flow is established, adding future apps follows the same pattern — making it repeatable and scalable across your organization.

Tags

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Business Application Studio, Fiori, RAP, ABAP Cloud, LADI, IAM App, Tile Configuration

entry.title: Develop RAP-Based SAP Fiori Applications in Minutes with SAP Fiori Tools AI Project Accelerator
entry.content:
Last year, as described in the blog From idea to app: using AI-powered Project Accelerator to develop SAP Fiori apps, the Project Accelerator focused on generating CAP-based projects,

Developing enterprise SAP Fiori applications using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a standardized way to build modern cloud-ready applications. These applications are typically developed in environments such as SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (version 2602 or higher).

In a significant step forward, the Project Accelerator now supports RAP-based projects In this post, I will explain why the addition of RAP is important and then describe the specific steps  to build an app using this new capability.

The addition of RAP support makes the AI Project Accelerator more relevant for enterprise SAP development by aligning it with SAP’s strategic ABAP model. It enables end-to-end generation of backend artifacts and frontend fully functional SAP Fiori elements application including manifest and annotation configurations. Furthermore, it supports the Clean Core 4-Level Extension Model, as the generated backend artifacts fall under Level A—the cleanest form of extensions.

The AI-powered Project Accelerator in SAP Fiori tools is available in SAP Business Application Studio (SBAS) under a SAP Build Code subscription ( is available).

Application ideas or business requirements can be provided by developers, designers, and business experts in various forms, such as:

  1. Text
  2. Image or UI mock-up (.jpg, .png)
  3. Combination of text and image as a business requirement document (.docx, .md)

The project accelerator enables rapid prototyping of a fully functional application that can be showcased and then extended using SAP Fiori tools or any coding assistants (such as Cline, Claude, and Copilot) integrated with MCP servers (for example, MCP server for SAP Fiori or CAP) for further development.

It generates the complete application stack from scratch, including the CDS data model, behavior definitions, service definitions, OData V4 service, and an SAP Fiori elements application. This follows a greenfield approach, with no reuse of existing business objects.

Getting Started

Now that we understand the AI Project Accelerator, let’s proceed with the step-by-step walkthrough to generate the RAP project using an SAP BTP trial account.

Before we begin, complete the following prerequisites:

  1. Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial (follow step 1 to step 5 and in step 6, choose region US East- AWS) . Note: The trial version of SAP Build Code is currently only available in the US East - AWS region.
  2. Start Booster- Prepare an Account for ABAP Trial (only step 1)
  3. Start Booster – Get started with SAP Build Code

Navigate to subaccount(trial) and verify Instances and subscriptions.

Create a Destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Manually add below additional properties to destination and continue to review step.

  • WebIDEEnabled: true
  • abap_enabled: true
  • WebIDEUsage: odata_abap,dev_abap,abap_cloud
  • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  • HTML5.SetXForwardedHeaders: false
  • HTML5.Timeout: 360000
Verify all the details of destination and click on create.    
 

Configure ABAP Development Tools (ADT) and create new ABAP package.

  1. You have installed ABAP Development Tools, latest version
  2. Create new ABAP Cloud Project.
  3. Copy URL from destination properties.
  4. Create new ABAP package Z_<Unique Name> under super package ZLOCAL

SAP Build Code - Create new dev space

  1. Open the SAP Build Code lobby.
 
 
 
Create new Dev space that contains SAP Fiori tools extensions in SAP Business Application Studio.
 
 
Provide a Dev space name (for example: FioriAppDevelopment).
 

Once the Dev space is created and starts running, click on FioriAppDevelopment to open SAP Business Application Studio

Launching the SAP Fiori Tools AI: Project Accelerator

 Go to Project Explorer view and Click on Open Folder, to open your workspace folder (/home/user/projects).

 
Open the Command Palette

Search for “Fiori application” and select “Fiori: Open Application Generator”.

 
For AI-assisted SAP Fiori app creation click on "Project Accelerator"
 
Select RAP Accelerator.
  • Select the destination.
  • Enter the ABAP repository name
  • Choose the package for back-end artifacts generation
  • Provide a transport request (if required)
  • Not needed for local packages
  • Click Connect to proceed.
 

Generating Your First SAP Fiori Elements Application

 The AI Project Accelerator supports multiple input formats. One powerful approach is generating SAP Fiori application from images.

I have a hand-drawn sketch representing a Manage Purchase Orders application. the expectation from the Project Accelerator is to interpret the visual design and underlying business intent, identify floorplan and generate a RAP based SAP Fiori elements application. The generated application should closely reflect the structure, fields, and layout depicted in the image, converting the sketch into a fully functional List Report and Object Page.

Download manage_purchase_orders.png to your local disk and upload it to the Projects Folder. Drag and drop the image to the projects pane on the left.

Click on Choose File and select the manage_purchase_orders.png

 

You can view the generation logs to understand how SAP Fiori tools AI generates your application and if needed, to gather additional details for troubleshooting. // Add as side-info

Click on Generate. The project accelerator will analyze the image and generate a, including back-end OData V4 service artifacts and front-end artifacts. 

For supported features and known restrictions, refer to the documentation

In ABAP Development Tools (ADT), check the destination system and ensure that all backend OData V4 service artifacts are generated and available in the selected package.

 

. 

 

After the generation process completes, the new project (e.g., ai-created-rap) is added to your workspace, and the Application Information page opens automatically.

On the Application Information page, under Common Tasks, click Preview Application.

  • Select start-mock to preview the application with AI-generated mock data.
  • Alternatively, use the start script to preview the application with the backend; however, the backend will not have any data as it is newly generated.

You will then see the SAP Fiori elements application with a List Report (including a filter bar and a table for purchase orders. Compare the generated SAP Fiori application with the uploaded image to verify how closely the project accelerator has interpreted the floorplan, fields, and structure.

.       

Overall, the AI Project Accelerator for RAP can significantly speed up the development of RAP-based SAP Fiori applications, enabling developers, designers, and business users to transform ideas, requirements, or even sketches into functional enterprise apps within minutes.

References:

Generating an Application with the Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI

Why use the AI-powered Project Accelerator from SAP Fiori tools?

SAP Fiori Tools Update: Elevating Development with AI, Usability, and Flexibility

entry.title: End to End Guide: Enabling SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad (PCE + OnPremise PoC)
entry.content:
1. Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI, designed to help users accomplish tasks through natural language interaction within SAP applications. This guide will provide a step by step walkthrough to enable joule in SAP Fiori.

The guide is primarily written for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition (PCE).
PS: Also covers steps that differ for On-Premise S/4HANA (for a Proof-of-Concept environment).

2. Component Overview

Before starting, it is important to understand the key components involved and the role each plays.

Component

What It Is

Role in This Integration

SAP Joule

SAP's generative AI copilot. A cloud service on SAP BTP.

The AI assistant end users interact with. Loaded in FLP header as a Shell Plugin.

SAP BTP

SAP Business Technology Platform  the cloud foundation.

Hosts Joule, Work Zone, Identity Services, and destinations connecting to S/4HANA.

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

SAP's digital workplace platform. Can serve as launchpad or content bridge.

Acts as the technical bridge between Joule and S/4HANA. Required in the Joule Formation.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

SAP's Identity Authentication Service. Manages user identity and SSO.

Provides the SSO layer. All BTP services require IAS authentication.

SAP Cloud Connector (SCC)

An on-premise agent creating a secure tunnel from BTP to on-premise systems.

Enables Work Zone and Joule on BTP to securely reach S/4HANA.

SAP Fiori Launchpad (FLP)

The S/4HANA web UI hub where users launch Fiori apps.

The surface where the Joule icon appears via a Shell Plugin target mapping.

Formation

A BTP System Landscape construct grouping systems for a specific integration.

Tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together.

CDM3

Common Data Model v3 . SAP Fiori's content model (catalogs, tiles, groups).

Used to expose S/4HANA Fiori content to BTP so Work Zone can sync it.

3. Architecture Overview

The following describes the end-to-end integration flow:

  • End User opens S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad in browser
  • FLP authenticates via IAS (after SSO is configured)
  • FLP loads the Joule Shell Plugin from the Joule Web Client (sapdas.cloud.sap)
  • Joule on BTP connects to Work Zone via the Joule Formation
  • Work Zone reaches S/4HANA via BTP Destinations → Cloud Connector → ABAP Server(S/4 system)
  • Joule icon appears in FLP header. Users interact with Joule through natural language.

4. Prerequisites

Requirement

Details

SAP BTP Global Account

Admin access. Subaccount in the same region as your IAS tenant.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

IAS tenant associated with your BTP Global Account.

SAP S/4HANA System

PCE or On-Premise (for PoC). FLP accessible via browser.

SAP Cloud Connector

Installed and running. Registered to your BTP subaccount.

S/4HANA Technical User

User with access to CDM3 service (/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3) and FLP admin roles.

BTP Entitlements

Joule (foundation), SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Regional Alignment

BTP subaccount, IAS tenant, and Work Zone must be in the same region.

Note: PCE vs On-Premise: PCE uses token-based registration via Communication Arrangements. On-Premise uses Cloud Connector and manual destinations. Each step calls out differences.

5. Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Enable Cloud Foundry in BTP Subaccount

Why: Cloud Foundry is the runtime where BTP service instances (Work Zone, Joule) are deployed. Without it, subscriptions and the Joule booster cannot run.

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit → navigate to your target Subaccount → Overview.
  2. Click Enable Cloud Foundry. Accept defaults (Plan: standard). Click Create.
  3. Once enabled, click Create Space → name it e.g. 'dev'. Assign yourself Space Developer + Space Manager roles.

Validation: Subaccount Overview shows CF Enabled, Org Name, API Endpoint, and space 'dev' listed.

On-Premise PoC: No difference. This step is identical for both PCE and On-Premise.

 

Step 2: Add Entitlements

Why: Entitlements grant your subaccount the right to use specific BTP services. Without them, the services cannot be subscribed to or instantiated.

  1. Go to Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments.
  2. Select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.
  3. Add: Joule (also search as 'das-application') — plan: foundation
  4. Add: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard
  5. Add: SAP Cloud Identity Services — plan: connectivity
  6. Click Save.

Validation: All three services appear in Entitlements list with quota assigned.

Step 3: Establish Trust to IAS

Why: All BTP services (including Joule) authenticate via IAS. Establishing trust allows your subaccount to delegate authentication to IAS, enabling SSO across all connected services.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration.
  2. Click Establish Trust.
  3. Select your IAS tenant (e.g., yourtenantid.accounts.ondemand.com).
  4. Click Next through the wizard → Finish.

Validation: Trust Configuration shows your IAS tenant with status Active.

Step 4: Configure Trusted Domains in BTP

Why: BTP Trusted Domains control which browser origins can interact with your subaccount's services via iFrames and cross-origin calls. Without this, the Joule panel cannot be embedded in your FLP.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Settings → Trusted Domains.
  2. Click Add for each of the following origins (scheme + host + port if non-standard):
  • Your FLP origin: e.g. https://your-s4-host:44301
  • Your Joule Web Client: https://<tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap
  • Your Work Zone site: https://<site-id>.launchpad.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
  1. Click Save.

Note: Your Joule URL is found in System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation → URL column of the Joule system entry.(You can maintain it later once Joule is activated via booster etc.)

Validation: All required origins appear in the Trusted Domains list.

Step 5: Configure Trusted Domains in IAS

Why: IAS maintains its own trusted domain allowlist. Cross-domain authentication requires IAS to trust the same origins as BTP.

  1. Open IAS Admin Console: https://<ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → Customization → Trusted Domains.
  3. Click Add — enter hostname only (no scheme or port) for each host.
  4. Click Save.

Note: Format difference: BTP accepts full origins (https://host:44301). IAS accepts hostname only (host.example.com).

Validation: IAS shows 'Trusted Domains updated' confirmation.

Step 6: Expose SAP Fiori Launchpad Content (CDM3)

Why: Work Zone needs to know what Fiori apps and catalogs exist in S/4HANA. The CDM3 export makes this content discoverable so Work Zone can sync it.

Pre-requisite: Activate CDM3 ICF Service

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction SICF.
  2. Navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. If inactive, right-click → Activate Service.

Expose Content

  1. Run transaction /n/UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE.
  2. Select Version 1.
  3. Choose All roles (first-time setup) or select specific roles.
  4. Click Expose Content in the toolbar. Wait for completion.
  5. Click View Log to verify results.

Note: Warnings in the CDM3 log are normal. Red errors should be investigated but typically do not block the integration.

Validation: Log shows entities exposed (e.g., '14,663 out of 14,671 entities exposed'). Click View Exposed Content to confirm.

Step 7: Set Up SAP Cloud Connector

Why: Cloud Connector creates a secure outbound-only tunnel from your on-premise/PCE network to BTP. Work Zone and Joule use this tunnel to reach S/4HANA without inbound firewall rules.

Please setup cloud connector first.

7.1 Register BTP Subaccount

  1. Open SCC Admin UI (e.g. https://localhost:8443).
  2. Add your BTP subaccount using Download Authentication Data from BTP → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors.
  3. Confirm status shows Connected (green).

7.2 Create Back-End Mapping

  1. Go to Cloud to On-Premises → + Add:
  • Protocol: HTTPS (preferred) or HTTP (PoC)
  • Internal Host/Port: e.g. S/4HANA internal hostname + HTTPS port 44301 or HTTP port 8001
  • Virtual Host: a clean alias (e.g., s4hana-virtual)
  • Back-End Type: ABAP System
  1. Add Resources: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3, /sap/bc/ui2/flp, /sap/bc/ui5_ui5 — all with 'Path and All Sub-Paths'.

Validation: Mapping shows green 'Reachable' status. BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors shows 'Available'.

On-Premise PoC: If HTTPS port 44301 is blocked between SCC and ABAP server, use HTTP port 8001 for PoC. In SICF, navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3 → Logon Data tab → check 'Use all logon procedures' to allow Basic Authentication.

Step 8: Activate Subscriptions and Instances

Why: Subscriptions activate the actual services in your subaccount. Without subscribing to Work Zone and Joule, neither the Joule icon nor the content bridge can function.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard.
  3. Subscribe to Joule (das-application) — plan: foundation.
  4. For Work Zone: also create a Service Instance (name: BuildZone) and a Service Key.
  5. Launch Work Zone from Instances & Subscriptions at least once — this registers it in System Landscape.

Note: The Joule Booster (next step) looks for the Work Zone subscription in its dropdown. It only appears after you launch Work Zone at least once.

Validation: Subscriptions show 'Subscribed'. Service Key created. System Landscape → Systems shows Work Zone auto-registered.

Step 9: Configure the Joule Formation

Why: A Formation is a logical grouping that tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together. Without a formation, Joule has no context about which systems to connect to.

Option A — Run the Joule Booster (Recommended for PCE)

  1. Go to Global Account → Boosters → search 'Setting Up Joule' → click Start.
  2. Wizard: Select subaccount and CF space (dev).
  3. Step 3 — Select Integrations: choose SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  4. Step 4 — Select Capabilities: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition + SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  5. Step 5 — Select your Work Zone system and S/4HANA system.
  6. Click Execute and wait for completion.

Option B — Add Systems to Existing Formation

  1. Go to Global Account → System Landscape → Formations.
  2. Find the formation of type 'Integration with Joule' with status Ready.
  3. Click Include Systems → add your Work Zone instance and S/4HANA system.
  4. Confirm integration options (Enable Capability Deployment, Enable Joule Icon).

Validation: Formation shows status Ready (green). Includes IAS, Work Zone, and Joule systems. Note the Joule URL — needed for Trusted Domains and shell plugin.

On-Premise PoC: On-Premise systems cannot complete Communication Arrangement token registration.

Recommended approach:

(1) Run the booster selecting only Work Zone as the integration (not S/4HANA).
(2) Register S/4HANA manually in System Landscape → Systems → Add → Type: SAP S/4HANA Cloud. (Not needed for POC)

Step 10: Configure BTP Destinations

Why: BTP Destinations define how Work Zone and Joule connect to S/4HANA. Without correct destinations, Work Zone cannot read content and Joule cannot reach the backend.

Create the following destination (Design-Time and Runtime can be the same for PoC):

Property

Value

Name

e.g, jouletc  (lowercase, no underscores)

Type

HTTP

URL

http://<scc-virtual-host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User / Password

S/4HANA technical user with CDM3 access

sap-client

Your S/4HANA client (e.g., 100)

HTML5.DynamicDestination

true

WebIDEEnabled

true

WebIDEUsage

odata_abap

 

Critical: Work Zone Destination Naming: Work Zone only accepts destination names that are lowercase with no underscores. Names like MCF_TC will cause an error. Use names like jouletc or s4hana.

Critical: URL Path: The destination URL must include the CDM3 path: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. Without this, Work Zone receives an HTML login page instead of JSON, resulting in an authorization error.

Validation: Check Connection returns HTTP 200. e.g. SWZ_JOULE destination is auto-created and visible in the destinations list.

 

Step 11: Configure Work Zone Channel Manager

Why: The Channel Manager connects Work Zone to S/4HANA as a Content Provider. This sync imports S/4HANA's Fiori catalogs and makes the app landscape available to Joule.

  1. Launch SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition from Instances & Subscriptions.
  2. Go to Channel Manager → + New.
  3. Fill in:
  • Title: your system name (e.g., S4H_UIB)
  • Design-Time Destination: your lowercase destination (e.g., jouletc)
  • Runtime Destination: same
  • Content Scope: Roles and related content
  • Automatically add all content items: ON
  1. Click Save, then click the sync icon.

Wait 5-15 minutes for first sync

On-Premise PoC: The channel may show 'Updated' with a 'Connectivity Error' in the Report. This error is for real-time change notification callbacks, which on-premise systems do not support. The content sync itself succeeds. Re-sync manually when S/4HANA content changes.

Validation: Channel shows Updated status. Content Manager → Explore confirms S/4HANA catalogs are visible.

Step 12: Configure Joule Shell Plugin in S/4HANA FLP

Why: The Joule icon in FLP is delivered via a SAPUI5 Shell Plugin configured as a Target Mapping in an FLP catalog. Without this step, users will not see the Joule icon regardless of BTP configuration.

12.1 Create Technical Catalog

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST (Launchpad Content Manager: Cross-Client).
  2. In the Catalogs tab, create a new catalog: ID = Z_JOULE_TECH, Title = 'Joule Technical Catalog'. Package: $TMP (PoC).

12.2 Create Shell Plugin Target Mapping

  1. Open catalog Z_JOULE_TECH in the Launchpad Designer (opens in browser).
  2. Click Target Map... tab → Create Target Mapping.
  3. Fill in:
  • Semantic Object: Shell
  • Action: plugin  (type manually — not in dropdown)
  • Application Type: SAPUI5 Fiori App
  • URL: https://<joule-tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/resources/public/webclient/s4  (NO trailing slash)
  • Title: Joule
  • ID: sap.das.webclientplugin.s4
  • Save.

Critical: No Trailing Slash: The Joule Web Client URL must NOT end with a slash. Use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/. A trailing slash causes 'File not found' and the icon will not appear.

Note: How to find your Joule URL: Go to System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation. The Joule system shows a URL like https://<tenant>.eu10.sapdas.cloud.sap. Append /resources/public/webclient/s4 to this base URL.

12.3 Create Role and Assign Catalog

  1. Run transaction PFCG → create single role Z_JOULE_S4HANA.
  2. Menu tab → click Launchpad Catalog → assign Z_JOULE_TECH (Local Front-End Server).
  3. Authorizations tab → Change Authorization Data → Do not select templates → Generate → Save.
  4. User tab → assign role to target users → User Comparison → Full Comparison → OK.

Validation: Log in to FLP. The Joule icon (sparkle/diamond) appears in the FLP header. Clicking it opens the Joule panel.

Step 13: Configure IAS SSO for S/4HANA FLP (Recommended)

Why: Joule is secured by IAS. If FLP uses ABAP local login, users must log in again when Joule opens. Configuring IAS SSO for FLP gives a seamless single login experience.

13.1 Download IAS Metadata

  1. Open IAS Admin Console → Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  2. Click Download Metadata File. Save the XML.

13.2 Configure SAML2 in S/4HANA

  1. Run transaction SAML2 in S/4HANA.
  2. Enable SAML 2.0 and create a Local Provider Name.
  3. Trusted Providers tab → Add → Upload Metadata File → upload IAS metadata.
  4. Set NameID format (Login Name recommended — maps to ABAP user ID).
  5. Download S/4HANA SP metadata.

13.3 Register S/4HANA as Application in IAS

  1. IAS Admin Console → Applications → + Add.
  2. Upload S/4HANA SP metadata under Trust → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  3. Set Subject Name Identifier to match your ABAP user attribute (Login Name).
  4. Save.

Validation: Opening FLP URL redirects to IAS login. After login, FLP opens without ABAP credential prompts. Joule opens without a separate login.

6. Troubleshooting

Symptom

Likely Cause

Fix

Joule icon not visible in FLP

Shell plugin not loaded or role not assigned

Verify role Z_JOULE_S4HANA is assigned. Hard-reload FLP (Ctrl+Shift+R). Check browser console for plugin errors.

'File not found' when Joule loads

Trailing slash in plugin URL

Remove trailing slash: use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/.

'Refused to frame/connect' error

Missing Trusted Domain entry

Add the exact blocked origin (shown in browser console) to BTP and IAS Trusted Domains.

Work Zone Channel: Connectivity Error

Real-time callbacks not supported (on-premise)

If status shows 'Updated' with Connectivity Error — content synced. Re-sync manually as needed.

Work Zone 'authorization error' on fetch

Destination URL missing CDM3 path

Ensure destination URL is http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3.

Booster fails: 'System already part of formation'

IAS can only be in one Joule formation

Find the existing Joule formation and add your systems to it instead.

SCC mapping 'Not Reachable'

Firewall blocking the port

Ask network team to open the port between SCC and ABAP server. Try internal IP instead of hostname.

Joule opens but prompts for login

IAS SSO not configured for FLP

Complete Step 13 — configure SAML2 trust between S/4HANA and IAS.

Work Zone destination name error

Name has uppercase or underscores

Rename destination to lowercase with no underscores (e.g., jouletc).

Wrong Joule URL after S/4HANA 2025 upgrade

DAS_WEBCLIENT_PLUGIN set to pilot URL

Update /UI2/FLP_SYS_CONF (SM30) with your tenant URL, or remove the parameter.

7. Summary — What We Achieved

  • Joule provisioned on SAP BTP and linked to IAS and Work Zone via a Formation
  • SAP Cloud Connector established as the secure bridge between BTP and S/4HANA
  • Work Zone Channel Manager syncing S/4HANA Fiori catalogs to BTP
  • BTP Destinations correctly routing Work Zone to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector
  • Joule Shell Plugin configured in S/4HANA FLP via a Technical Catalog and PFCG role
  • Joule icon visible and functional in the S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad

entry.title: Dynamic Criticality in SAP CAP Using Fiori Elements and Node.js
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a CAP application recently, I came across a requirement where records needed to be highlighted based on their status. The business users wanted a quick way to identify whether an order was completed, still in progress, or blocked without having to read through each record individually.

My first thought was to store a criticality value directly in the database, but that didn't feel like the right approach because the value was purely UI-related and depended on the business status. After exploring a few options, I found that CAP and Fiori Elements provide a clean way to calculate criticality dynamically in the backend and display semantic colors automatically in the UI.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to achieve this using SAP CAP, Node.js, OData annotations, and Fiori Elements. By the end, you'll have a working example where criticality is calculated at runtime and reflected directly in your Fiori application.

Why Use Dynamic Criticality?

In many business applications, the visual status of a record depends on changing business conditions. For example, an order may move from "In Process" to "Completed", or an expense request may be "Approved" or "Rejected".

Storing criticality values in the database means the values must be maintained whenever the business status changes. By calculating criticality dynamically in the CAP service, the UI always reflects the latest business state while keeping the database model focused on business data.

Step 1: Create a CAP Project

mkdir criticality-demo 
cd criticality-demo 
cds init 
npm install 
npm install sqlite3

Step 2: Create the Data Model

Create the file db/schema.cds

namespace demo.criticality;

entity SalesOrders {
  key ID           : Integer;
      CustomerName : String(100);
      Amount       : Decimal(10,2);
      Currency     : String(3);
      Status       : String(30);
}

Step 3: Add Sample Data

          Create the file:  db/data/demo.criticality-SalesOrders.csv

ID;CustomerName;Amount;Currency;Status
1;ABC Corporation;1200.00;INR;Completed
2;Global Traders;5000.00;INR;In Process
3;Tech Solutions;800.00;INR;Blocked
4;Amazon;900.00;INR;In Process

Step 4: Create an OData Service

          Create the file srv/service.cds.

using demo.criticality as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity SalesOrders as projection on db.SalesOrders;
}

Step 5: Configure Fiori Annotations

          Create the file app/annotations.cds.

using CatalogService as service from '../srv/service';

annotate service.SalesOrders with @(
  UI.LineItem : [
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Customer',
      Value : CustomerName
    },
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Amount',
      Value : Amount
    },
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Status',
      Value : Status,
      Criticality : Criticality,
      CriticalityRepresentation : #WithIcon
    }
  ],

  UI.SelectionFields : [
    CustomerName,
    Status
  ]
);

          The most important configuration is:

Criticality : Criticality

This tells SAP Fiori to use the Criticality value exposed by the OData service to determine the color and icon displayed for the Status field.

Step 6: Create a Fiori Elements Application

Open the SAP Fiori Application Generator and select:

  • Template: List Report Object Page
  • Data Source: Use a Local CAP Project
  • Service: CatalogService
  • Entity: SalesOrders

Complete the wizard to generate the Fiori Elements application.

Step 7: Run the Application

Start the CAP application:

cds watch

Step 8: Make Criticality Dynamic Using Node.js

Since Criticality is calculated dynamically, we do not need to persist it in the database. Instead, we expose it as a computed field in the service projection and populate it at runtime based on the business status.

A useful side effect of this approach is that if the business decides to change the criticality rules in the future, only the CAP service logic needs to be updated, without requiring database changes or frontend modifications.

At this stage, we will update the service definition created in Step 4 to expose a computed Criticality field.

using demo.criticality as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity SalesOrders as projection on db.SalesOrders {
    *,
    null as Criticality : Integer
  };
}

           Then Create srv/service.js:

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
  this.after('READ', 'SalesOrders', (orders) => {
    const list = Array.isArray(orders) ? orders : [orders];

    for (const order of list) {
      switch (order.Status) {
        case 'Completed':
          order.Criticality = 3;
          break;
        case 'In Process':
          order.Criticality = 2;
          break;
        case 'Blocked':
          order.Criticality = 1;
          break;
        default:
          order.Criticality = 0;
      }
    }
  });
});

With this implementation, criticality is calculated at runtime based on the business status rather than being stored in the database.

As shown below, Fiori automatically renders different semantic colors based on the Criticality value calculated in the CAP service. Completed orders appear in green, In Process orders appear in yellow, and Blocked orders appear in red.

Understanding Criticality Values

SAP Fiori uses the following standard criticality values:

Criticality Value Meaning Color Typical Usage

0NeutralGrayDefault or informational status
1NegativeRedError, Rejected, Blocked
2CriticalOrange/YellowWarning, Pending, In Process
3PositiveGreenSuccess, Approved, Completed

Conclusion

What I liked about this approach is that the database remains focused on business data while the CAP service handles the UI-specific logic. Instead of maintaining criticality values in a table, we can derive them dynamically from the status and expose them through the service whenever the data is requested.

One thing that took me some time to figure out was that the calculated field must also be exposed in the service projection. Without that step, the application receives the status value but not the criticality information needed for the semantic colors.

Although this example uses sales order statuses, the same pattern can be applied to approval workflows, expense reports, support tickets, or any scenario where users need a quick visual indication of the current state of a record. I hope this helps anyone looking to implement dynamic criticality in their own CAP applications.

entry.title: Using Domain Fixed Values as Value Help in SAP RAP Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP RAP Fiori applications, value help improves the user experience by providing predefined selectable values for filter and input fields.

One simple and efficient approach is to use ABAP Domain Fixed Values as value help instead of creating separate CDS value help entities.

In this blog, we will see how to implement value help in Fiori Elements using Domain Fixed Values in SAP RAP.

Business Requirement

In a SAP RAP Fiori Elements application, users should be able to select predefined status values through Value Help instead of entering values manually.

The Status field should display fixed values maintained in an ABAP Domain, such as:

  • 100 – Not Prepared
  • 101 – In Preparation
  • 102 – Completed

    When the user opens the value help in the Fiori application, the system should automatically display these fixed domain values along with their descriptions.

    Step 1:  Create a Domain with Fixed Values

    Before building the Value Help CDS View, we first need to create an ABAP Domain that contains the fixed status values.

    The domain will act as the source for the Value Help values displayed in the Fiori application.

     
    Step 2: Build the Value Help CDS View
    SAP provides two standard tables that expose domain fixed values:
       1. DD07L  - Contains the fixed values of the domains.
       2. DD07T - Language-dependent description texts.
    We join these two views and pass our domain name as a parameter. The join on `$session.system_language` is important — without it, you'd get one row per language in the system, causing duplicates in the dropdown.
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Status'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZI_BP_STATUS_VH 
as select from dd07l as values
    inner join   dd07t as texts on  values.domname    = texts.domname
                                and values.domvalue_l = texts.domvalue_l
                                and texts.ddlanguage  = $session.system_language
{
      @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'StatusText' ]
  key values.domvalue_l as StatusCode,
      texts.ddtext      as StatusText
}
where values.domname = 'ZDO_VD_STATUS'
and texts.domname = 'ZDO_VD_STATUS'.

 

Step 3: Attach the Value Help to the Main CDS View

Now go to the Projection View (Consumption CDS View) where the Status field is defined.

Use the annotation @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition to connect the field with the Value Help CDS View.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@Search.searchable: true
@ObjectModel.semanticKey: [ 'ExternalId' ]
define root view entity ZC_BP_REQHDR
  provider contract transactional_query
  as projection on ZI_BP_REQHDR
{

  key     RequestUuid,

          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          ExternalId,
          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'RequesterName' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'I_BusinessPartnerVH',
                                                         element: 'BusinessPartner' } }]
          RequesterId,
          _Requester.BusinessPartnerName as RequesterName,
          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'StatusText' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZI_BP_STATUS_EDIT_VH',
                                                          element: 'StatusCode' } }]
          Status,
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'StatusText' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZI_BP_STATUS_VH',
                                                         element: 'StatusCode' } }]
          //          Status                         as StatusFilter,
          _status.StatusText             as StatusText,
          StatusCriticality,
          @search.defaultSearchElement: true
          @ObjectModel.text.element: [ 'PriorityText' ]
          @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZI_BP_PRIORITY_VH',
                                                         element: 'PriorityCode' } }]
          @Consumption.filter.selectionType: #SINGLE
          Priority,
          _priority.PriorityText         as PriorityText,
          DeadlineDate,
          CancelReason,

          @ObjectModel.virtualElement: true
          @ObjectModel.virtualElementCalculatedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_BP_CANCEL_REQUEST'
  virtual CancelReasonHidden : abap_boolean,

          LastChangedAt,

          _items : redirected to composition child ZC_BP_REQITEM,
          _Requester
}

 

Value Help Configuration in RAP

The below annotations are used to enable Value Help for the Status field in SAP RAP Fiori Elements.

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition

This annotation connects the field with a CDS Value Help entity.

ZI_BP_STATUS_VH

This CDS View acts as the Value Help provider and supplies the fixed domain values.

element: 'StatusCode'

Specifies which field from the Value Help entity should be returned to the main field.

@ObjectModel.text.element

This annotation helps display the description text together with the code value in the Fiori UI.

With this configuration, users can select predefined status values directly from the Fiori Value Help dialog instead of entering values manually.

 

Step 4: Expose the CDS View in Service Definition

After creating the Value Help CDS View and Projection View, the next step is to expose the CDS entity through a Service Definition.

@EndUserText.label: 'Request Services'
define service ZUI_BP_REQUEST {
  expose ZC_BP_REQHDR;
  expose ZC_BP_REQITEM;
}

 

Output

After activating the CDS Views, Service Definition, and Service Binding, the Status field in the Fiori Elements application will display a Value Help popup containing the fixed values maintained in the ABAP Domain.

 

Conclusion

In this blog, we saw how to implement Value Help using ABAP Domain Fixed Values in a SAP RAP Fiori Elements application without creating custom database tables or complex Value Help logic.

By using the standard tables DD07L and DD07T, we were able to:

  • Expose domain fixed values directly in the Fiori UI
  • Display meaningful descriptions alongside technical keys
  • Improve the user experience with predefined selections
  • Avoid unnecessary custom Value Help entities
  • Build a lightweight and reusable solution

This approach is simple, efficient, and easy to maintain. Any changes made to the domain fixed values are automatically reflected in the Fiori application without additional development effort.

I hope this blog helps you implement Value Help efficiently in your SAP RAP applications.

entry.title: Freight Order Execution Monitoring
entry.content:
Freight Order Execution Monitoring app gives KPI based view of Freight Orders, so they do not need to open each document manually. From the monitoring screen, users can drill down into detailed execution status and take action where needed.

The app helps Dispatcher users to:

  • Monitor Freight Orders by execution status
  • Identify delayed or incomplete Freight Orders
  • Compare planned and actual execution updates
  • Review missing arrival or departure events
  • Support smoother settlement and reporting

This app is very useful for daily transportation operations. If execution events are missed, it can create issues later in charge calculation, settlement and tracking carrier payment. By using this app regularly, teams can catch execution gaps early and keep the Freight Order process transparent.

Activity On Time Performance:


Carrier On Time Performance:

Conclusion
The Freight Order Execution Monitoring app gives better visibility and control over the Freight Order lifecycle. It helps operations teams focus on exceptions, improve follow up, and ensure execution updates are completed before settlement processes begin.

Reference:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/e16bfa31fde2409a8422e36844ffc143.html?utm_source=chatgpt.comhttps://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/e16bfa31fde2409a8422e36844ffc143.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: Building an Inventory System with RAP on BTP Trial — CDS Views Explained
entry.content:
Introduction

When people talk about RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming Model), the conversation usually jumps straight to behavior definitions, actions, and determinations. The CDS layer gets a brief mention and everyone moves on.

That's a mistake.

The CDS view architecture is the contract your entire application is built on. Get it right and everything above it — behaviors, OData services, Fiori UIs — falls into place naturally. Get it wrong and you'll spend hours debugging problems that were actually design issues.

In this post I'll walk through the complete CDS layer of a Mini Inventory & Stock Movement System I built on SAP BTP Trial, covering four entities across three business objects. I'll explain the design decisions, not just the code.

The System at a Glance

The application manages warehouse stock movements. Three business objects cover the full domain:

Business Object Interface View Database Table
Material MasterZI_MiniMM_MaterialZRMZ_MM_MATERIAL
Storage LocationZI_MiniMM_StorageZRMZ_MM_STORAGE
Stock Document (Header)ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentZRMZ_MM_DOC_HDR
Stock Document (Item)ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItemZRMZ_MM_DOC_ITEM
Current Stock (read-only)ZI_MiniMM_CurrentStockZRMZ_MM_STOCK

Each entity has a matching projection view (ZC_ prefix) used to expose the OData service.

The Two-Layer Architecture

RAP enforces a clean separation between two CDS view types. Understanding why this separation exists is more important than understanding the syntax.

Interface views (ZI_) are the authoritative source of truth. They sit directly on the database tables and carry everything the framework needs to understand your data: semantic annotations, associations, compositions, and quantity/currency references. These views should be stable — changing them has downstream impact.

Projection views (ZC_) consume the interface views and add presentation concerns: the provider contract, @Metadata.allowExtensions for metadata extensions, and the redirection of compositions so that OData navigation points to the correct projection layer. Projection views can evolve more freely.

A useful mental model: ZI_ is the API contract, ZC_ is the UI adapter.

Composition: Stock Document Header and Item

The most important structural relationship in this model is the header-item composition between ZI_MiniMM_StockDocument and ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem.

In the header (root entity):

 
 
abap
composition [0..*] of ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem    as _Items

In the item (child entity):

 
 
abap
association to parent ZI_MiniMM_StockDocument  as _Header  on $projection.DocumentID = _Header.DocumentID

The [0..*] cardinality means a document can have zero or more items — appropriate here since a newly created document starts empty. The parent association on the item side creates the back-navigation link.

Why does this matter? Composition in RAP is not just a modelling convenience. It has runtime implications: the managed runtime treats the composition tree as a single transactional unit. When you delete a header, items are automatically deleted. When you lock a header for editing, the entire tree is locked. This is the correct behaviour for a stock document.

Associations: Two Aliases, One Target View

The item view introduces an interesting challenge: a stock movement can have both a source storage location and a target storage location, and both must be validated against the same ZI_MiniMM_Storage entity.

The solution is two separate associations pointing to the same underlying view:

 
 
abap
association [1..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Material as _Material  on $projection.MaterialID = _Material.MaterialID
association [0..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Storage as _SourceStorage  on $projection.SourceStorage = _SourceStorage.StorageID
association [0..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Storage as _TargetStorage  on $projection.TargetStorage = _TargetStorage.StorageID

A few design points worth noting here.

The _Material association is [1..1] — every item line must reference a valid material. _SourceStorage and _TargetStorage are both [0..1] because not every movement type uses both. A goods receipt (GR) has no source; a goods issue (GI) has no target. Making both optional at the CDS level correctly reflects the business reality.

The alias names (_SourceStorage, _TargetStorage) are different even though both navigate to ZI_MiniMM_Storage. This is required by the framework — CDS does not allow two associations with identical names on the same view, even if the target differs.

Semantic Annotations: Free Administrative Data

RAP's managed runtime will auto-populate administrative fields if you annotate them correctly. No custom code required.

 
 
abap
@Semantics.user.createdBy: truecreated_by      as CreatedBy,

@Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: truecreated_at      as CreatedAt,

@Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: truelast_changed_by as ChangedBy,

@Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: truelast_changed_at as ChangedAt

These annotations go on the interface view, not the projection. The framework reads them at runtime and fills the mapped database columns on every create and update operation.

The only requirement is that the underlying database table columns have compatible data types (SYUNAME, TIMESTAMPL respectively) and that you declare with_draft or standard managed behaviour in the behavior definition. If those conditions are met, this is genuinely zero-effort audit trail data.

Projection Views: What Most Tutorials Skip

Every interface view has a corresponding projection view. The projection view adds three things that the interface view cannot provide: the provider contract transactional_query declaration (required for OData V4 exposure), @Metadata.allowExtensions: true (required for metadata extensions to attach), and — crucially — the composition redirect.

The composition redirect is the step that most beginner tutorials either skip or explain poorly.

In the projection header:

 
 
abap
_Items : redirected to composition child ZC_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem

In the projection item:

 
 
abap
_Header : redirected to parent ZC_MiniMM_StockDocument

Without this redirect, OData navigation from the header to its items would traverse back to the interface entities (ZI_), not the projection entities (ZC_). The service binding would still activate, but navigation in Fiori would either fail silently or return data without the metadata annotations your UI depends on.

Value Help Annotations

The final piece of the CDS layer is the value help definitions, which live in the metadata extension of the item projection view.

 
 
abap
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Material',
  entity.element: 'MaterialID' }]MaterialID;
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Storage',
  entity.element: 'StorageID' }]SourceStorage;
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Storage',
  entity.element: 'StorageID' }]TargetStorage;

The annotation always points to the projection view (ZC_), not the interface view. This is because the value help dropdown in Fiori is itself an OData call, and OData services are generated from projection views. Pointing at ZI_ would fail at activation or produce no results.

Both SourceStorage and TargetStorage reference the same ZC_MiniMM_Storage projection — same entity, same element, different field. The framework handles this correctly.

Running This on BTP Trial

Everything described in this post runs on a free SAP BTP Trial account with the ABAP environment service instance. No S/4HANA system required. No license. The ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse is the only tool you need locally.

If you've been waiting for the right time to start learning ABAP Cloud and RAP, a BTP trial account is genuinely the right place to start. The constraints of the cloud environment (no classic ABAP, no access to SAP standard tables directly) force you to learn the correct patterns from the beginning.

ArchitectureMaterial View InterfaceMaterial Create InterfaceStock Movement Interface

entry.title: SAP Cloud ERP Launchpad Extension v3.2 – Dark Mode, Auto-Login & More
entry.content:
What is the SAP Cloud ERP Launchpad Extension?

A quick recap for new readers: this extension (available for Chrome, Edge, and Firefox) lets you save your favourite SAP Fiori apps and Cloud ERP system URLs directly in the browser toolbar. Instead of navigating through bookmarks or typing URLs manually, you open the extension popup, click your app, and you're there. Read more here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/can-a-free-chrome-extension-transform-your-sap-cloud-erp-experience/ba-p/13801414

 

🌙 Dark Mode

The most visually impactful change: the extension now supports a full dark mode, consistent across both the popup and the options page.

To switch it on, open the Options page (click the extension icon → settings, or right-click → "Extension options") and toggle the Dark Mode switch in the footer bar.

The dark theme applies to:

- The extension popup (tree view, buttons, footer bar, search field)

- The full Options page (tables, dialogs, tab headers, the shell bar)

Your preference is saved automatically and persists across browser restarts.

 

🔑 Auto-Login with Saved Credentials

You can now save a username and password for each of your Cloud ERP systems. When you navigate to a saved system's SAP Fiori Launchpad for the first time, the extension automatically fills in the login form for you — no typing needed.

How to save credentials

  1. Open the Options page
  2. Go to the Cloud ERP Systems tab
  3. Click Create to add a new system, or select an existing one and click Edit
  4. Fill in the optional Username and Password fields
  5. Click Save

 

The password is stored securely in your browser's sync storage and is shown as "Saved" in the table — the actual value is never displayed again for security.

How auto-login works

 

Once credentials are saved, the extension's background service will detect when you open a matching SAP system URL and automatically inject your username and password into the login form. This works seamlessly in the background — you just arrive at the Fiori Launchpad already logged in.

 

> Note: Credentials are stored only locally in your browser and are never transmitted anywhere. When you export your Cloud ERP Systems list, the download file contains only the name and host URL — credentials are intentionally excluded to protect your privacy.

 

Adding Cloud ERP Systems with Login Credentials Directly from the Popup

The popup now includes Username and Password fields when adding a Cloud ERP System through the Add → Cloud ERP System menu.

 

🔒 Privacy-Safe Export

When you download your Cloud ERP Systems list (Download button in the footer of the Options page, while on the Cloud ERP Systems tab), the exported JSON file now contains only the name and host URL for each system.

Usernames and passwords are never included in exports. This means you can safely shareyour system list with colleagues without worrying about credential exposure.

 

🦊 Firefox: Fixes and Improvements

Several improvements have been made specifically for Firefox users:

- Tree duplication fixed: A Firefox-specific bug caused the Fiori Apps tree in the popup to render items twice. This was caused by Firefox firing browser storage callbacks more than once under certain conditions. The issue has been fully resolved.

- Upload fixed: The file upload (importing JSON) now works correctly in Firefox.

- Translations: All new field labels (Username column, Password column, "Saved" status) are now fully translated across all 18 supported languages in the Firefox version.

 

Download and Install

Chrome:

https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/sap-cloud-erp-launchpad/nelmoakcfgfgkigcjgkmibhmgfpbhcbh

Edge:

https://microsoftedge.microsoft.com/addons/detail/fiori-launchpad-for-sap-c/kfbfaeokdhfbeikhofemebocbbfcmpcb

Firefox:

https://addons.mozilla.org/de/firefox/addon/sap-cloud-erp-launchpad/

 

Documentation:

https://edrilanberisha.github.io/sapclouderplaunchpad/

 

Feedback Welcome

As always, this extension is built for the SAP community. If you find bugs, have feature requests, or just want to say the dark mode made your day — please leave a comment below or open an issue on GitHub.

Happy navigating! 🚀

entry.title: Security Administrator Exam
entry.content:
Planning on taking the SAP C_SEC certification? Here are a few tips that may help with your preparation:

  • Pay close attention to SAP naming conventions. Many exam questions rely on the exact terminology used by SAP, so understanding names, roles, objects, and concepts is essential.

  • Read each question carefully—more than once if needed. A single word can change the meaning of the question and lead to a different answer.

  • Think outside the box. Some questions are scenario-based and require you to apply your knowledge rather than simply recall facts.

  • Review the Live Sessions, Learning Journeys, and training materials thoroughly. They cover the key topics and concepts you'll need for the exam, so don't skip them.

Good luck with your preparation and the exam!

#saphana#fiori

entry.title: Stop Users from Typing Invalid Values in SAPUI5 ComboBox – A Practical Validation Approach
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of my recent SAPUI5 projects, I needed users to select a value from a predefined list. Since the list was quite large, I wanted to make the selection process as smooth as possible. Instead of using sap.m.Select, I went with sap.m.ComboBox because it lets users search for values by typing, making it much easier to find the right option.

Everything worked as expected until I started writing unit test cases. While testing different scenarios, I noticed that users weren't limited to selecting values from the dropdown they could also type any value they wanted. Even if the entered text didn't exist in the list, the ComboBox would still accept it.

It might seem like obvious behavior once you know how ComboBox works, but it was something I hadn't considered during development. That's when I realized that while the control offers a great user experience, it doesn't automatically guarantee valid input.

We could have used a value help dialog for selection, but the requirement specifically called for a dropdown-based experience. That’s why the focus stayed on ComboBox  and a deeper validation approach around it.

In this article, I'll walk through the approach I used to keep the search functionality intact while ensuring users can only submit values that actually exist in the dropdown.

 

The Real Issue in Applications

In actual business scenarios, this becomes a real problem when data moves to the backend. Users may enter free text in the ComboBox and it still gets treated as valid input even if it doesn’t exist in the master data.

This can lead to invalid payloads, failed backend validations, and inconsistent business data in reports. What starts as a small UI behavior can quickly turn into a data integrity issue.

That’s why, when using sap.m.ComboBox for strictly controlled fields like Country, Plant, or Company Code, frontend validation becomes essential before processing the input.

 Picture 1: Understanding the ComboBox and It's Behaviour

 

The Approach I Used

To handle this, I added a simple validation on the ComboBox events. The idea was straightforward—accept the input only if it matches a valid item from the list.

I mainly relied on the change event along with getSelectedKey(). If the user entered something that didn’t match any item, I cleared the value, showed an error state using setValueState("Error"), and displayed a meaningful message.

I also used selectionChange to reset the error state when a valid option was selected from the dropdown, ensuring a smoother user experience.

 

Implementation

The validation was handled mainly using the change and selectionChange events. The goal was to ensure that only valid values from the dropdown are accepted, while giving clear feedback when something incorrect is entered.

XML View Code: (.xml file)
<!-- XML View -->
<ComboBox
    id="countryCombo"
    items="{path: '/Countries'}"
    selectionChange=".onSelectionChange"
    change=".onChange">
    <core:Item
        key="{CountryCode}"
        text="{CountryName}" />
</ComboBox>
Controller Code: (.js file)
// Controller Logic
onChange: function (oEvent) {
    const oCombo = oEvent.getSource();
    const sValue = oCombo.getValue().trim();
    const sKey = oCombo.getSelectedKey();
    if (!sValue || !sKey) {
        oCombo.setValue("");
        oCombo.setValueState("Error");
        oCombo.setValueStateText("Please select a valid value from the list.");
        return;
    }
    oCombo.setValueState("None");
},

onSelectionChange: function (oEvent) {
    oEvent.getSource().setValueState("None");
}

 

Best Practices

When working with sap.m.ComboBox, validation should always be treated as part of the design, not an afterthought.

Use getSelectedKey() instead of relying only on getValue(), since free text input is always possible. Trim user input before validation to avoid whitespace-related issues.

Always provide clear ValueState feedback so users immediately understand what went wrong. Also, reset the error state on valid selection using selectionChange to improve usability.

Finally, even if frontend validation is in place, always enforce the same checks at the backend to ensure data integrity.

 Picture 2: Data Integrity

 

Alternative Approaches

Depending on the requirement, there are a few other ways to handle this.

If search is not critical and the list is small, sap.m.Select is a simpler option since it only allows predefined selections.

In some cases, developers try disabling free typing in ComboBox, but that removes the search flexibility, which is often the main reason for choosing it in the first place.

Another option is model or backend-level validation, but that should always complement frontend checks rather than replace them.

  Picture 3: Right Approaches for Handling Selections

 

Key Takeaway & Conclusion:

A ComboBox is not automatically restricted to its items. If your requirement is to allow only predefined values, you must explicitly validate the user input before processing it.

The goal is not to remove the flexibility of sap.m.ComboBox, but to balance it—keep the search experience intact while ensuring only valid, business-approved values make it to the backend.

Kindly Note: We could have used a value help dialog for selection, but the requirement specifically called for a dropdown-based experience. That’s why the focus stayed on sap.m.ComboBox and a deeper validation approach around it.

If you have any alternative effective solutions, please feel free to share your thoughts and so everyone can learn from each other.

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! If you found this helpful, i would love to hear your thoughts, feedback or questions in the comments. Let's keep learning and growing together!

I’m still new to blogging, so if you notice anything that could be improved or corrected, please don’t hesitate to let me know!!

entry.title: Your Voice on the Future of SAP UI Technology
entry.content:
AI is reshaping how enterprise applications are built, used, and experienced. This raises fundamental questions for SAP's UI technology strategy - and we want to hear from you.

TL;DR: We are running a short, anonymous survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It takes 5 minutes and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026. Take the survey →

What are we asking?

We have put together a short survey on the future of enterprise UI development with SAP. It covers topics such as:

  • How well SAPUI5 meets your needs today - and in the age of AI
  • Which UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise apps in the coming years
  • Which capabilities matter most for your future development work
  • Where you see SAP's UI technology strategy heading

Why your input matters

SAP's UI frameworks power thousands of enterprise applications worldwide. Decisions about their future direction affect you — whether you are building new apps, maintaining existing ones, or advising customers on their architecture. Your perspective as a developer, architect, or decision maker in the SAP ecosystem is what we need to make the right calls.

Who should take this survey?

This survey is for anyone working with SAP UI technologies: SAPUI5 / Fiori developers, UI and UX architects, technical leads, enterprise architects, and IT decision makers at SAP customers and partners.

Call to Action

Make your voice heard by participating in the survey here: SAP UI Technology Strategy Survey. It only takes 5 minutes to complete, and is open from July 1 to July 19, 2026.

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge — Snake & Ladder with SAP CAP Status Transition Flows
entry.content:
SAP Developer Challenge - Snake & Ladder with SAP CAP Status Transition Flows

Welcome to the July 2026 SAP Developer Challenge — a 4-week hands-on series where you build a multiplayer Snake & Ladder game as a fully functional SAP CAP service, powered by CAP Status Flows (@flow.status).

The Scenario

We will be building the backend engine for an online Snake & Ladder game. Each player's position on the board is a state — and every move, snake bite, and ladder climb is a status transition modelled using CAP's @flow.status annotations.

By the end of July, you'll have:

  • A running CAP Node.js service with CDS entities and status-flow bound actions
  • A multiplayer game engine with turn management, dice rolls, snake/ladder triggers, and CAP events
  • A SAP Fiori UI displaying the live board — participants just plug in their service URL
  • Integration with the CAP MCP server to let an AI assistant drive moves via natural language

What Makes This Interesting

CAP's @flow.status feature gives you out-of-the-box:

  • Pre-validation — a @from: [#Playing] annotation rejects any action called in the wrong state (HTTP 409), no custom code needed
